title: {file}
brief: Detailed report of current Element F findings and analysis.

elements.label: Gantt Chart Showcasing the Team's Progress
Problem Statement
elements.content: Commercially available STEM toys designed for children—particularly those marketed towards girls—often fail to sustain inquiry-driven engagement through narrower technical content, limited opportunities for authentic engineering practices, and inconsistent value relative to gender-neutral alternatives. These shortcomings reduce opportunities for meaningful guardian-child co-play and can dampen long-term interest in STEM among children ages 6-12. The M.O.J.O. team's proposed solution, a modular rover-based construction kit, addresses these documented gaps through the deliberate integration of evidence-based design principles, iterative engineering practices, and authentic STEM content across every stage of the development process.

elements.label: Introduction
elements.content:
Element F represents a critical checkpoint in the M.O.J.O. team's engineering design process, requiring a systematic evaluation of whether the proposed modular rover kit should continue toward production and distribution. Having completed two documented prototype revisions, materials and fabrication research, and a full application of STEM principles in Element E, the team is now positioned to assess the broader viability of the design from ethical, technical, and market perspectives. The rover prototype is constructed using FDM 3D-printed structural components in PLA/ABS filament, a single 18650 lithium-ion cell managed by a TP4056 charging circuit, dual N20 DC motors driven by a DRV8833 motor driver, and an ESP32 microcontroller programmed in C++ via the Arduino IDE. Design requirements established in Element C — including a retail price target under $60, minimal-tool assembly under 30 minutes, a runtime exceeding 45 minutes per charge, line-following performance above 85% track completion, and drop resilience from 0.8 meters — continue to serve as the measurable benchmarks against which the prototype is evaluated.
The dual-packaging strategy, neutral aesthetics, and structured co-play framework embedded in the design reflect direct responses to expert guidance from Dr. Grace Paradis and Dr. Nancy Dayne, both child development specialists who emphasized the critical role of parental involvement and gender-neutral presentation in sustaining children's STEM engagement. Evidence gathered through consumer surveys, patent research, peer-reviewed literature, and three expert interviews collectively supports the conclusion that a genuine market need exists for this product and that the M.O.J.O. team has assembled the technical and domain knowledge necessary to responsibly advance the design.

elements.label: Should the Product Be Developed?
elements.content:
The M.O.J.O. team concludes that the modular rover kit should be developed. The justification for continued development rests not merely on technical feasibility, but on documented social need and evidence of genuine market demand. Consumer survey data collected in October 2025 demonstrated that 61.1% of respondents believe STEM toys are not marketed equally toward boys and girls, while 60.8% consider STEM toys highly important for child development. This disconnect between recognized importance and inequitable market offerings represents a concrete problem that the rover kit is designed to address. Furthermore, peer-reviewed literature reviewed in Element A corroborates that many products labeled as girls' STEM toys underdeliver on authentic inquiry, relying instead on stereotyped themes and consumable activities that offer limited long-term educational value.
The rover kit's mechanics-first design, gender-neutral aesthetics, and structured co-play framework represent a principled response to this documented gap rather than a novelty product seeking market share. The team is therefore confident that the question of "just because you can, doesn't mean you should" is answered affirmatively — the product addresses a real, evidence-backed problem with a purposeful and ethically grounded design.

elements.label: Has the Team Acquired the Necessary STEM Knowledge?
elements.content:
The M.O.J.O. team has systematically acquired and applied the science, mathematics, and engineering knowledge necessary to responsibly design the rover prototype. From a science standpoint, the team evaluated thermoplastic material properties — including glass transition temperatures, tensile strength, and impact resistance — across PLA, PETG, and ABS filaments to inform structural material selection. Electrical science was applied through Ohm's Law and the power formula to design a regulated multi-rail power delivery system, ensuring that the ESP32 microcontroller received a stable 3.3V supply and the N20 motors received a regulated 6V, rather than an unregulated and potentially damaging direct battery connection. Electrochemical principles governing lithium-ion discharge were used to calculate a theoretical runtime of 6.25 hours under conservative assumptions, far exceeding the 45-minute specification established in Element C.
From a mathematics standpoint, dimensional tolerance calculations were performed throughout the CAD modeling process to account for the ±0.1–0.5mm variability inherent in FDM fabrication, with clearance offsets stored as named parameters in Autodesk Fusion 360 and propagated automatically across all affected geometry. The TP4056 charge rate formula was applied to confirm a theoretical recharge time of 2.5 hours at the default 1A charge current, validating the product's reusability claim. From an engineering standpoint, the team maintained a structured engineering notebook, updated a Gantt chart at regular intervals to track milestone completion, and applied an iterative design methodology across two documented prototype revisions in which failures identified in Revision 1 were logged and directly translated into geometry and slicer parameter corrections for Revision 2. Technology tools including Autodesk Fusion 360, UltiMaker Cura, IdeaMaker, and the Arduino IDE were used throughout in a manner consistent with professional engineering workflows.

elements.label: What Do Experts Say? — Dr. Grace Paradis
elements.content: Dr. Grace Paradis, Ph.D., Assistant Professor of Child Development at California State University Stanislaus, was interviewed on September 23, 2025. Dr. Paradis emphasized that children do not inherently differ in STEM ability, and that observed disparities arise from socialization and cultural expectations rather than biology. She recommended neutral or mixed-color design to avoid reinforcing stereotypes and suggested a dual marketing strategy — offering both a gender-neutral and a girl-targeted package with identical educational content — to maximize market reach. Dr. Paradis further stressed that cost-effectiveness and safety are primary factors in parental purchasing decisions, recommending a retail price target in the $20–$40 range, and highlighted the importance of co-play guidance that lowers the barrier to parental engagement without requiring STEM expertise.

elements.label: What Do Experts Say? — Dr. Nancy Dayne
elements.content: Dr. Nancy Dayne, Ed.D., Professor of Child Development at California State University Long Beach, was interviewed on September 30, 2025. Dr. Dayne reinforced that biological differences in STEM learning between boys and girls are not supported by evidence, and that packaging and color cues strongly shape parent and child perception before play begins. She placed particular emphasis on parental involvement, noting that parents not only make the purchase decision but also serve as the primary mediators of how deeply a child engages with a toy over time. Dr. Dayne stressed the importance of delivering authentic educational value rather than relying on the broad "STEM" label, which she noted is applied indiscriminately across products of widely varying quality.

elements.label: What Do Experts Say? — Jasmine Shields
elements.content: Jasmine Shields, a Software Engineering student at UC Irvine, was interviewed on January 19, 2026. Shields provided technical guidance on the rover's firmware and electronic systems. She supported the team's decision to use C++ on the ESP32 via the Arduino IDE, affirming that this approach aligns with industry-standard embedded systems practices and produces consistent, reliable behavior compared to block-based coding environments, which she identified as unpredictable and likely to produce frustration in student-facing projects. She noted that features such as line following and AGV behavior are not purely software problems and depend critically on appropriate hardware and sensor integration. Shields also flagged battery capacity as a practical constraint as additional sensors and motors increase total current draw, and confirmed that operating the system while charging introduces safety risks that the team has already addressed through the TP4056's CC/CV charging profile and the design decision to prohibit simultaneous charging and motor operation.

elements.label: Laws, Codes, and Regulations
elements.content:
Several regulatory frameworks are relevant to the M.O.J.O. rover kit and must be considered as the design moves toward potential production. The Consumer Product Safety Improvement Act (CPSIA) establishes federal safety standards for children's products sold in the United States, including restrictions on lead content, phthalates, and mandatory third-party testing requirements for products intended for children under 12. The rover kit's target age range of 6–12 places it squarely within CPSIA jurisdiction, meaning any production version would require certified laboratory testing prior to retail sale. The Federal Hazardous Substances Act (FHSA) governs labeling requirements for products containing potentially hazardous materials; the 18650 lithium-ion cell in the rover qualifies as a regulated energy storage device and would require appropriate warning labeling regarding charging, storage, and disposal.
Battery transport and shipping are additionally governed by IATA and DOT regulations for lithium-ion cells, which impose restrictions on shipping state of charge and packaging requirements. From an electrical safety standpoint, UL certification or equivalent third-party listing would likely be expected by major retailers for any product incorporating rechargeable battery systems. Finally, FCC Part 15 regulations would apply if wireless communication features such as Bluetooth or Wi-Fi were added to the rover, as Jasmine Shields noted during her expert interview — a consideration the team used to justify deferring wireless features beyond the current prototype scope.

elements.label: Ethical Responsibilities
elements.content:
The M.O.J.O. team believes it has met its ethical responsibilities in relation to the design of this product. The rover kit is intended for children ages 6–12, a population that demands heightened attention to safety, inclusivity, and developmental appropriateness. All electronic components operate below 9V, eliminating meaningful shock risk during normal use. The TP4056 charging circuit implements overcharge protection, and the design explicitly prohibits simultaneous charging and motor operation as documented in Element E's power delivery block diagram. The team's use of ABS filament for structural components prioritizes impact resistance over printability convenience, directly serving the safety of the child end user.
From an inclusivity standpoint, the mechanics-first design language and dual-packaging strategy reflect a deliberate ethical commitment to ensuring that no child is implicitly excluded from STEM engagement by the product's branding or aesthetics. The team also recognizes the ethical dimensions of material choice: PETG produces lower VOC emissions during printing than ABS, reducing occupational health risk during classroom fabrication. At end of life, the lithium-ion cell requires responsible e-waste disposal, and the team has documented this in the product life cycle diagram included in this element. Expert consultation with child development specialists ensured that the design decisions affecting the target population were informed by domain expertise rather than assumption.

elements.label: Potential Failure Modes and Mitigation Strategies
elements.content:
The team has identified several plausible failure modes and has incorporated specific design features to minimize their likelihood and impact.
Structural failure under drop loading is the most likely mechanical failure mode given the target age group. The 0.8-meter drop resilience specification established in Element C was selected specifically to approximate table height, a realistic accident scenario in classroom and home use. PETG was selected over PLA as the preferred structural material due to its significantly higher impact resistance (3.0 ft-lb/in versus 0.4 ft-lb/in for PLA), reducing the probability of brittle fracture on impact. Infill density for load-bearing components was set to 40% in the slicing software to maximize isotropic impact resistance.
Sensor failure or misreading represents the primary electronic failure mode for the line-following behavior. Environmental factors including ambient lighting variation and track surface reflectivity can produce inconsistent color sensor readings. The team addressed this by specifying a standardized test track — black electrical tape on white matte paper — for all formal performance testing, and by using the Arduino IDE's serial monitor to calibrate sensor thresholds during bench testing before integration into the full system.
Electronic connection failure due to repeated assembly and disassembly is mitigated by the use of keyed and polarized JST connectors throughout the electronic system, preventing incorrect mating and reducing mechanical stress on solder joints. All connectors were selected to prevent incorrect assembly without requiring technical knowledge, consistent with the design's accessibility goals.
Battery depletion mid-session is addressed by the theoretical runtime calculation documented in Element E, which confirmed a conservative estimate of 6.25 hours of operation from a single 2,500 mAh 18650 cell — well in excess of the 45-minute specification. USB-C compatibility with standard household chargers eliminates the need for proprietary charging hardware and ensures recharging is accessible to all families.
Finally, firmware instability or unexpected rover behavior is mitigated by the decision, reinforced by Jasmine Shields, to use C++ on the ESP32 rather than block-based programming environments, which were identified as producing inconsistent and unpredictable results in student-facing applications. The structured mode-switching architecture — separating manual drive, line-following, and obstacle avoidance into distinct operational tiers — reduces the risk of unintended behavior during mode transitions.

elements.label: Project Merit
elements.content:
The M.O.J.O. modular rover kit demonstrates value that extends well beyond the classroom by addressing a problem documented in peer-reviewed academic literature, validated by child development experts, and confirmed through original consumer research. Coyle and Liben (2020) demonstrated through controlled research with 61 mother-child dyads that gendered toy packaging produces measurably different learning outcomes and caregiver mediation patterns, confirming that the design decisions embedded in the rover's dual-packaging strategy carry genuine educational consequences. Alexandre et al. (2022) identified caregiver involvement as a consistent lever for sustained STEM engagement across a systematic review of 36 studies, directly validating the team's co-play framework as evidence-based rather than incidental. A product built on this foundation is not a classroom exercise — it is a response to a documented gap in the educational toy market supported by the same research base that informs child development policy.
The team's October 2025 consumer survey provides quantitative evidence that demand for this product exists. Of respondents, 60.8% rated STEM toys as highly important for child development, yet 61.1% simultaneously recognized that toys are not marketed equally toward boys and girls — a clear signal that the market is failing a consumer base that already understands the value of what it is not being offered equitably. Affordability was identified as the top purchasing priority by 44.2% of respondents, and 83.6% reported having building sets as children, indicating strong receptivity to construction-based play among the parent demographic who will make the purchasing decision. These figures represent real, measurable demand rather than assumed need.
The current competitive landscape further supports investment in this product. Existing competitors either sacrifice educational depth for affordability, impose excessive cost without commensurate learning value, or rely on gendered aesthetics that limit their audience. No currently reviewed competitor simultaneously delivers modularity, gender-neutral design, structured co-play support, authentic sensor-based engineering content, and a retail price accessible to most families. The rover kit occupies a differentiated and currently underserved position in this market, with a projected at-scale unit production cost under $25 supporting a retail price in the $30–$50 range that is both competitive and commercially viable.
The pathway from prototype to market is realistic and follows the established trajectory of comparable STEM toy brands. Direct-to-consumer online sales, consistent with how GoldieBlox and Yellow Scope initially reached their audiences, represent a feasible entry point before potential expansion into educational suppliers and mainstream retail. The dual-packaging strategy enhances discoverability by allowing the product to appear in both gender-neutral and girl-targeted toy sections simultaneously without requiring separate inventory. Two documented prototype revisions, a full bill of materials analysis, and expert validation from three consultants across child development and software engineering collectively demonstrate that the investment of time and resources into this project is grounded in evidence and directed toward a product with genuine commercial and educational potential.

elements.label: Lifecycle Diagram
elements.target: EDD/[W3B] EDD Element F 2.0 & 3.0 Design Review and Project Merit

elements.label: Conclusion
elements.content:
Element F represents the M.O.J.O. team's systematic evaluation of whether the modular rover kit should continue toward production, and the evidence assembled across this element consistently supports that it should. The design review confirmed that the team has acquired and applied the science, mathematics, and engineering knowledge necessary to responsibly develop the product, as demonstrated through material property analysis, electrical circuit design, tolerance calculations, battery runtime verification, and two documented prototype revisions grounded in structured iterative methodology. Expert consultation with Dr. Grace Paradis, Dr. Nancy Dayne, and Jasmine Shields collectively validated the team's core design decisions — neutral aesthetics, dual packaging, structured co-play scaffolding, C++ firmware on the ESP32, and a regulated multi-rail power delivery system — while identifying realistic obstacles including regulatory compliance requirements, battery constraints, and sensor-hardware dependencies that the team has actively addressed in the current design. Relevant laws and regulations including the CPSIA, FHSA, and DOT lithium-ion shipping requirements have been identified and will need to be formally satisfied should the project advance to commercial production, representing known and manageable compliance obligations rather than insurmountable barriers.
The team is confident that its ethical responsibilities have been met at this stage of development. Safety considerations including low-voltage operation, overcharge protection, impact-resistant material selection, and responsible end-of-life documentation have been embedded into the design from the outset rather than treated as afterthoughts. The inclusivity principles at the heart of the product — mechanics-first branding, gender-neutral aesthetics, and accessible co-play guidance — reflect a genuine commitment to serving a population that existing market offerings have consistently underserved. Consumer survey data, peer-reviewed literature, and expert testimony together provide compelling evidence that a real and currently unmet demand exists for this product, and that the investment of time and resources into its continued development is justified. Looking ahead to Element G, the team will conduct formal prototype testing against the measurable specifications established in Element C, analyze results using the scientific and mathematical frameworks documented in Element E, and use testing outcomes to guide any remaining design refinements before finalizing the product concept.
